stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \). The matrix \( B \) is obtained from \( A \) by adding \( [[k]] \) times the first row to the second row. What is \( \det(B) \)?
solution: Adding a multiple of one row to another row leaves the determinant unchanged, so \( \det(B) = \det(A) = ([[a]])([[d]]) - ([[b]])([[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the determinant by \( [[k]] \), which is the effect of scaling a row, not adding a multiple of one row to another.
This adds \( [[k]] \) to the determinant; a row-addition operation adds nothing.
This is \( -\det(A) \), the effect of a row swap.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \). The matrix \( B \) is obtained from \( A \) by adding \( [[k]] \) times the first column to the second column. What is \( \det(B) \)?
solution: Adding a multiple of one column to another column leaves the determinant unchanged (the same rule that holds for rows also holds for columns), so \( \det(B) = \det(A) = ([[a]])([[d]]) - ([[b]])([[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the determinant by \( [[k]] \), which is the effect of scaling a column, not adding a multiple of one column to another.
This adds \( [[k]] \) to the determinant; a column-addition operation adds nothing.
This is \( -\det(A) \), the effect of a column swap.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

solution: A matrix with an entire row of zeros always has determinant 0, since every term in the cofactor expansion along that row is 0: \( \det(A) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the zero row as if it contributed a nonzero cofactor term.
This sums entries from the nonzero first row, ignoring that a zero row forces the determinant to vanish.
This computes a leftover minor from the two nonzero rows instead of recognizing the zero row forces \( \det(A)=0 \).
A matrix with a zero row cannot have a nonzero determinant.
A matrix with a zero row cannot have a nonzero determinant.
This computes a minor of the nonzero rows rather than the determinant of the full matrix.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[r21]] & [[r22]] & [[r23]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] & [[f]] \end{bmatrix} \), whose second row is \( [[m]] \) times its first row. What is \( \det(A) \)?
solution: Two proportional rows make the rows linearly dependent, so the determinant is always 0 regardless of the third row: \( \det(A) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakes the proportionality factor \( [[m]] \) itself for the determinant.
This computes a leftover minor as if the proportional rows did not force the determinant to vanish.
This computes a nonzero-looking combination instead of recognizing that proportional rows force \( \det(A)=0 \).
Two proportional rows always force the determinant to 0, never to \( \pm1 \).
Two proportional rows always force the determinant to 0, never to \( \pm1 \).
This treats the third row as if it could rescue a nonzero determinant despite the dependent first two rows.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] & [[f]] \\ [[r31]] & [[r32]] & [[r33]] \end{bmatrix} \), whose third row equals the sum of the first two rows. What is \( \det(A) \)?
solution: Since row 3 is a linear combination of rows 1 and 2, the rows are linearly dependent, so \( \det(A) = [[answer]] \) regardless of the specific entries.
distractor_rationales: This computes a leftover product as if the linear dependence among the rows did not force the determinant to vanish.
This sums entries instead of recognizing a row that is a combination of the others forces the determinant to 0.
This is the \( 2\times2 \) minor of the first two rows, not the determinant of the full dependent matrix.
A matrix with a row equal to a combination of the others always has determinant 0, never \( \pm1 \).
A matrix with a row equal to a combination of the others always has determinant 0, never \( \pm1 \).
This multiplies the (dependent) third row's entries together rather than recognizing the determinant vanishes.
